planting.note: Plant container-grown divisions in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; bird of paradise blooms most prolifically when the roots are slightly restricted — avoid overpotting or overly large planting holes. Plants from seed take 3–5 years to bloom; divisions flower within 1–2 years.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; bird of paradise is one of the most iconic and recognizable flowering plants in the world, producing dramatic flowers of vivid orange and electric blue that resemble the head of a tropical bird emerging from a boat-shaped green spathe — each flower head lasts several weeks and established clumps produce flowers nearly year-round in frost-free gardens. A well-established clump of 5–7 crowns in a sunny location in zone 9–12 is one of the most spectacular things in any garden. The broad, paddle-shaped leaves on long petioles provide bold architectural foliage between bloom cycles. Slightly root-restricted plants bloom far more freely than those planted in oversized holes — this is one plant that thrives in a somewhat confined root zone. Water deeply but infrequently once established and apply a slow-release balanced fertilizer in spring and again in midsummer. Divide large clumps in spring for propagation, taking care to minimize root damage.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring or fall in any moist to dry, well-drained to moderately wet, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade to deep shade; cast iron plant is essentially indestructible in zones 7–11 and establishes in conditions where almost nothing else will grow — deep shade, root competition, poor soil, drought, and even flooding are all tolerated.

directions: Plant in any soil in full shade to deep shade — cast iron plant lives up to its common name as the most tolerant, indestructible landscape plant available for zones 7–11. No other evergreen perennial survives deep shade, drought, root competition, salt, flooding, neglect, and poor soil with the same equanimity. The broad, lance-shaped, glossy dark-green leaves (12–24 inches long) emerge from ground-level rhizomes and form a dense, weed-suppressing clump that deepens and spreads slowly over years. It is the premier plant for impossible spots: the north side of the house, under overhanging eaves, beneath dense magnolias or live oaks where nothing else grows. Variegated forms ('Milky Way' with white speckles, 'Asahi' with white-tipped leaves) add more visual interest. The plant grows very slowly — increase your initial spacing to 18 inches and know it will take 3–5 years to fill in. Once established, it is permanent: decades-old plantings of cast iron plant are commonly found thriving with zero maintenance.

planting.note: Plant pups (offsets from the mother rosette) or container-grown plants in spring in extremely well-drained, sandy to rocky, alkaline to neutral, lean soil in full sun; agave requires perfect drainage above all else — it will rot quickly in clay soil or where water pools. Plant slightly above grade to improve drainage.

directions: Plant in full sun in extremely well-drained, lean, rocky or sandy soil — perfect drainage is the only critical requirement; century plant is one of the most dramatic and architectural plants available for hot, dry landscapes and can survive on rainfall alone in appropriate climates once established. The large blue-gray rosette of thick, succulent leaves with sharp terminal spines and hooked marginal teeth grows slowly over 10–30 years before producing a single towering flower stalk (15–30 feet tall) laden with thousands of yellow flowers — then the main rosette dies, leaving behind offsets (pups) that perpetuate the clump. The juice of the leaf base was fermented by indigenous peoples of Mexico to make pulque; the young flower stalk was roasted and eaten as a sweet vegetable. Despite the name 'century plant,' blooming typically occurs after 10–30 years, not 100. Outstanding as a bold specimen and accent plant for xeric landscapes; handle with caution due to the sharp terminal spine which can cause serious injury.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container plants in spring in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; copper plant establishes quickly in warm weather and roots readily from stem cuttings. Pinch growing tips regularly during establishment to encourage a bushy, compact habit.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; copper plant is one of the most spectacularly colorful tropical foliage plants for zone 9–12 landscapes, with large, heart-shaped leaves in a remarkable range of copper, red, orange, bronze, green, and bicolored combinations — the foliage color is as vivid and jewel-like as any flower. The hundreds of named cultivars include types with solid copper-red leaves, mottled green-and-red, white-edged, and brilliantly painted multicolor forms. It grows rapidly into a large, bold shrub and is used extensively as a colorful screening plant, hedge, or specimen in tropical and subtropical landscapes. The best foliage color develops in bright light or partial shade — deep shade reduces color intensity. Regular fertilization during the growing season is important for maintaining vigorous growth and best color. In zone 9 it may suffer frost damage in cold winters but typically regrows from the base; in zone 10–12 it is evergreen and persistent.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican heather establishes quickly and begins blooming almost immediately after transplanting. In zones 9–10 it often self-sows freely and may behave as a short-lived perennial; it is used as an annual in zones 8 and colder.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Mexican heather is a fine-textured, dense-growing tropical perennial that produces a continuous profusion of tiny lavender, pink, or white flowers on compact mounds of tiny, shiny, narrow leaves — resembling true heather (Calluna) in growth habit and foliage texture but adapted to tropical heat rather than cool climates. It blooms almost without pause from planting through frost and is one of the best butterfly and skipper nectar plants available for Gulf Coast gardens. In zone 9 it is typically a short-lived perennial that often reseeds to maintain a permanent presence. It is outstanding as a low edging plant, in mass plantings, along pathways, or at the front of mixed borders where its fine texture contrasts beautifully with coarser tropical foliage. Requires almost no deadheading or maintenance; simply trim lightly in early spring to refresh the habit.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants or rooted cuttings in spring in any well-drained soil in full sun; trailing lantana is exceptionally drought and heat tolerant and establishes rapidly. Unlike L. camara, it rarely self-seeds invasively in zone 9 and is generally less aggressive in landscapes.

directions: Plant in full sun in any well-drained soil — poor, sandy, and rocky soils are ideal; trailing lantana is a low-growing, wide-spreading groundcover form of lantana that smothers slopes, spills over walls, and covers large areas with a continuous carpet of small lavender-rose to purple flower clusters from spring through frost. Unlike the more common Lantana camara (upright lantana), trailing lantana (L. montevidensis) has a naturally prostrate, cascading growth habit making it superb for slopes, raised beds, and retaining walls where it can drape downward. It is more frost-hardy than upright lantana, less invasive in seed production, and almost indestructible once established. In zone 9 it often persists as a semi-evergreen perennial year-round. An outstanding butterfly and pollinator plant — particularly attractive to skippers and small native bees. Prune back by one-third in late winter to prevent woody buildup and encourage fresh, dense regrowth.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes 1 inch deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic, well-drained to moderately moist soil in partial to full shade; peacock ginger thrives in bright indirect light or filtered shade and actually struggles in full sun in hot climates. It goes completely dormant in winter — mark its location to avoid disturbing rhizomes.

directions: Plant rhizomes in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial to full shade; peacock ginger is one of the most beautiful and unusual tropical perennials for shaded Gulf Coast gardens, producing large, flat, spreading leaves marked with an intricate silver-green pattern (resembling peacock feathers) that lie almost flat on the ground and create a fascinating low carpet in the shade garden. Small but lovely white and pale purple flowers emerge directly from the leaf surface in late spring and summer, nestled among the ornamental foliage. The aromatic rhizomes are used medicinally in Southeast Asian traditional medicine and as a culinary spice (known as 'kencur' in Indonesian cuisine). The plant is completely dormant from late fall through early spring — all top growth dies back, but the rhizomes persist in the ground and emerge vigorously when warm weather returns. An outstanding understory plant for the shaded tropical garden in zones 8–11.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant rooted divisions or stem sections in spring or fall in any moist, well-drained soil in full shade to full sun; greater periwinkle is extraordinarily adaptable and establishes quickly from stem sections placed in moist soil. It spreads aggressively by arching stems that root at the nodes — contain with edging where spread must be controlled.

directions: Plant in any well-drained to moderately moist soil in full shade to partial sun; greater periwinkle is a vigorous, trailing, evergreen groundcover with larger, glossier, darker green leaves than the more refined Vinca minor — it covers ground more aggressively and thrives in the heat and humidity of zones 7–11 where V. minor can struggle. Sky-blue, star-shaped, 1.5-inch flowers appear from early spring through summer. Particularly valuable in the hot Southeast and Gulf Coast where its heat tolerance far exceeds V. minor. The variegated form ('Variegata', with leaves edged in creamy white or yellow) provides striking year-round foliage interest in shaded spots. Be aware that V. major can be invasive in riparian zones and warm-climate areas where it spreads into native plant communities — do not plant near natural areas or waterways in warm climates. Excellent for covering slopes, under trees, and in difficult shaded areas in contained residential landscapes.

planting.note: Plant rooted stem cuttings in spring after temperatures are consistently above 60°F in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; chenille plant roots easily and establishes quickly in warm weather. Pinch growing tips regularly to encourage dense, bushy growth and maximum flower production.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; chenille plant is one of the most unusually beautiful tropical flowering plants, producing long, pendulous, fuzzy catkin-like flower spikes in brilliant crimson-red that dangle like fluffy chenille tassels 12–18 inches long — an unmistakable and astonishing floral display unlike anything else in the garden. The flowers are produced nearly year-round in frost-free zones. It thrives in the warm, humid conditions of zone 10–12 and is used as a summer annual or large container plant in cooler zones; in zone 9 it may be grown as a seasonal annual or overwintered in a greenhouse. Bright filtered light or morning sun produces the most flowers while preventing leaf scorch in the afternoon heat. Regular watering and monthly fertilization during the growing season keeps the plant vigorous and blooming well. Propagate from 6-inch stem cuttings in late spring.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture

planting.note: Plant rhizome divisions in spring after temperatures are reliably above 65°F in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic, humus-rich, fertile soil in partial shade to full sun; heliconia requires consistently warm temperatures to thrive — avoid planting while soil remains cool. Divide clumps in spring every 2–3 years to maintain vigor.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; heliconia is one of the most exotic and spectacular tropical plants available for zone 9–12 gardens, producing large, bold banana-like foliage and dramatic flower bracts in brilliant shades of red, orange, and yellow that attract hummingbirds prolifically. H. psittacorum (parrot heliconia) is one of the smaller and most adaptable species, with slender upright stems 3–5 feet tall and colorful upright flower bracts in red-orange with green and yellow tipping — produced nearly year-round in frost-free gardens. The flowers are outstanding in tropical cut flower arrangements. Heliconias require consistent heat, moisture, and humidity to thrive; they are not drought tolerant and decline quickly when chilled below 50°F. In zone 9a they may suffer frost damage to foliage but regrow from rhizomes in spring if the soil does not freeze. Apply a thick layer of mulch to protect roots during cold snaps.
sources: University of Hawaii College of Tropical Agriculture
University of Puerto Rico Agricultural Extension Service
